response: The internet traces its origins to 1960s U.S. Department of Defense-funded computer networking research, which yielded ARPANET—first demonstrated in 1969 with a message between UCLA and Stanford Research Institute. In 1983, the adoption of TCP/IP protocols laid the modern internet’s technical foundation, while Tim Berners-Lee’s 1989 CERN-invented World Wide Web (with HTML, URLs, and HTTP) and 1993’s Mosaic browser made the previously academic/military-only technology accessible to ordinary users. Commercial and societal turning points followed: the late 1990s dot-com boom brought massive internet company investment, a 2001 crash eliminated most of these firms, but survivors like Amazon and Google became some of history’s most valuable companies; subsequent 2000s innovations included 2006’s AWS (launching cloud computing as a major force), mid-2000s social media (Facebook, Twitter, YouTube) that redefined global communication, and 2007’s iPhone launch that popularized ubiquitous mobile internet. Today, the internet connects over 5 billion people worldwide, serving as the foundational infrastructure for nearly every facet of modern life, from commerce and communication to entertainment.
